speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Emmett Utt has a colorful way with words and he has some strong convictions. Born near where he lives still, outside of Princeton, Emmett worked for many years with saws in the Potlatch Mill. Here he talks of early logging and mining and some about milling and travel. He tells stories of certain attitudes of Potlatch Forest management that got them into trouble and he explains our foreign relations by a story about the relations between a coyote and a dog.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Everybody would go out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well today they sure would.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: So, but that's the difference. I got to know him. He was just a congressman. But boy, he'd come out there, you know, and everybody, why he was royalty, you know. Everybody just would buy, you know. Oh boy, if a congressman came out here in this country today, there wouldn't probably be anybody turn off the road to go see him. That's the difference in the way government is today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they listen to what he said?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Do you remember what he said? Oh yeah, I forget now what he said. He was just another human being. He wasn't a superman or nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I wonder if he talked about, if he talked about politics or if he just talked about what a nice country he was.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, he was talking about himself and where he went to school here and different people he knew here. And, yeah, he just talked for a little while, you know. But he was a local boy, made good, and that was awful unusual to have a punk, you know, go next to the woods back to Washington D.C. You know, that was really out of this world. Pretty sure all the people who used to run the government was Eastern people in them days. Of course they always had their representatives from Idaho and Washington, all right, but I don't know. It's just odd the way he come out here by then and people, the town of Troy was just loud enough with people. They were just walking on one another's feet around there. And he had a stand built out there and got up and made a speech. I bet there was 5,000 people around me. Oh, it must have been that many, even that more. But, yeah, the old log schoolhouse over here at Princeton. Well, there's no school here at all. Now they tore everything down. But originally I started the school myself in that little log schoolhouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's where you went to school, where you went to school.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Over here at Princeton.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, that's where you went.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yes, that's where I went. I went my first year in that log house. But they was building the other schoolhouse, the same building. They was building it, but they had the first two, three grades, I think, in school in that little log house. I remember my first teacher was named Miss McCann. That was quite a day that first day of school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened the first day of school?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, they had the main building built. There was a whole school in the new school that was built there. There was a whole school in here. But they had a professor named Oakley Hall. Oakley Hall, he lived in Moscow. He lived there. He died a few years ago. But he was a professor in upper grades. So, I don't know if you remember them old desks. They had the iron frame down and had a hole about that big around right down the floor. You remember them?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think I've seen them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I didn't have nothing to do. So, I sat there and I kept my foot working in that hole. So, as far as I got my whole foot through it, well, I couldn't get it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No kidding.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And school mom, she come and she twisted this way with my foot and I guess she couldn't get my foot out of there. So, as far as she had to go and get the old professor, Oakley Hall would come out. Boy, he was mad the first day of school, you know. He was busy in there. He'd come and plop it in there. He grabbed my foot and he twisted this way and it wouldn't go. Finally, I take my shoe off, get my foot out. I tell you, I was just kidding. That man looked ten foot tall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, boy.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: He just scared me to death. It was the devil coming. That was just getting me any worse. So, that was my first day of school. I got a little log out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, man, that must have made a lasting impression.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, it did. I'll never forget that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I don't know. Yeah, I don't know. A lot of things happen around here, I guess. A lot of things went on around here in early days. People won't believe nowadays. You know, you start telling things that happen. They look at you like they all have you. Way out yonder, you know. Well, for instance, one winter, we had the porphyry of snow on the level. All over the valley here. More than that up in the woods. The porphyry level, the fence was all covered over. Well, then it rained. Got quite a shower. And then that night, it clearly just froze. It all froze across that thick on top of that porphyry of snow. And, uh, they used a log. I don't know if you remember, you've seen them old, big loggin' bobsleds. Big, what they call loggin' bunks. Well, they'd pile them up just like logging on them. Just like they do these short truck launches. All the 16 foot logs, you know, they'd pile them up there. And they'd put four or six horses ahead of it. And haul it down into the river, and they'd dump them over into the river. Roll them over the bank. If they could, it would float the bank they could get. All up and down the river. Decks of logs. Every man had his certain pot to put a log. Of course, you had your logs branded. You know how they're branded logs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With an iron? With an iron?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, it's an iron. It's a sledgehammer. And maybe your number would be old. Or it could be E, C, anything. There'd be a C at the end of that hammer. And you'd go along and hit the end of the log with that hammer. And then put that number there. That'll actually be your number. So they went down the scale down to Millwire. You get credit for your log. And so like I say, they was hauling them all up and down the river. If they had the main road run right down here, it blew the barn. It just blew this barn mine. And went across that fat man's place there. And they headed straight to the river. But when that snow come and it got that deep and froze, they'd probably have six head of horses on them sleds. Because they wanted to land the log way down yonder someplace. They rode it over here. They'd just swing them horses up on top of that, all that snow. And the sled loaded logs would go up on top of it. And they'd go over there on top of it for a bit of snow. With a sled loaded log. Six hundreds on it. Never breaks through. That's kind of hard to believe. But they sure did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how is it different with all the ice? Just because of the ice cover they could... How's that? Just because of the ice cover they could...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well crusts. You see crusts is snow. Oh yeah. Well that's what it all was. It just come right on top of all that snow. And it turned cold and froze. And that froze so hard that by a guy's set, the horses and the thing go out on top of the snow. Right over the top of the fence and everything.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Don't get in the weather like that no more. Haven't had any weather. I don't know since. We used to... Check this out. It's all changed. Every winter, they'd log up here. In the summer they'd deck the logs up here. Hundreds of thousands of feet of logs would be decked up. You know what a deck of logs is. Oh yeah. Well they'd be decked along the road. There'd be a big deck here and there. And they'd deck them all in the summertime and they'd wait for the snow to come. And then they'd haul them to the river. Then the spring when the high water come, they'd roll them to the river and they could head off the pot patch. That's the way they got the logs down to the mill. Floated down the river.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That was a great time.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Get...

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Can I have a cup of coffee?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sure. Thank you.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, and...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That's the way they got the logs down to the mill. Oh no, I've seen them up here by a guy. Fall big trees on that crutch. And they'd have to dig down around the stump. In order to leave your stump so high, you know. They'd dig down around it and then saw the tree down. And then fall over on that crutch of snow. And then my dad did up here in the field up on the central place up there. Falling big old pine trees down, you know. And then of course when they fell, they crushed the snow. They'd get down and cut the limbs off as much as they could on it. Except the bottom of it. Put a chain around it and put the horse down on top of the snow out there. They'd roll that log out of the bed fairly up on top of the snow. Of course my dad hauled them off of what they call a good ammo. Just a two runner rig. And... Just two runners with a bunker across it. And they didn't have a... Usually just had a... Five or six feet long between the runners. And then a chain would run through that business. And so the runners could flex. And go around the curb by one runner and take a head of the other. Seesaw. They called them good ammo. They was a devil.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would the guy... Would he just go... Where would he be on the...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: He'd set up on top of the log and drive his horses. In the wintertime when the logs were wet they had a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He'd sit right on the logs? Huh? Sit right on the logs?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, no. Usually in the wintertime when it was bad weather they'd... Well, they always had what they... Gunny sack full of straw. As they called them dry asses. And they'd put that on set-offs, you know. That's what they called them. The name of that damn sack was the dry ass. Well, they kept getting wet.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, but they used to... They never failed. I've never seen them fail to get all the logs in during the winter. On dial-a-ways, you know, get the deck log that way for snow to come. You never will get them in, you know. You probably will get them in the morning. But that's not to get maybe one or two trips when the snow's gone. Uh-huh. We haven't had any snow here to... Slay right on for years. Maybe a day or two it gets... I've seen 10 inches, 12 inches. A couple days it's gone. That's all there was. There's about 20 more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they work... Did they used to work all winter?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they'd haul logs all winter. They'd work all summer getting them decked up in the hills. And in the wintertime, they'd wait for the snow to come. And then they'd start hauling logs and dumping them in the river. I guess some places when the snow wasn't too deep, they'd log in the wintertime. But they usually figured on cutting them in the summertime and decking them, then hauling them in the winter. And they never failed to get them in. They always had enough snow to get them all in. Uh-huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So did the mill run all year round?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had plenty to keep them going.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. Well, of course, they got to building the railroad further up. They built the railroad potlights from the blues to start with. And that's when they... All this country was logged off by just hauling them to the river. And then floating them down the river. That's the way this country on here was all logged off. But then they built the railroad on up to Bovill. And, well, they went to Harvard first. Then they went from Harvard on up to line to Bovill. This was the railroad. And, of course, then they got to log and haul them in on the railroad. And they got the railroad in. They always load them on the railroad, haul them down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean they stopped... they used that instead of the river? Huh? Instead of the river?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, yeah, there's a lot of logging down here. They used to be a railroad run. This road you go up on here, Brinton out?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Now, that was the old railroad bend. They had spur running up here, run, slip, to take them up different canyons. Oh, yeah, they used to run trains right up through this canyon up here. Shays, they call them. Wasn't a railroad. What they call a shade. You know what a shade engine is? Well, it's a... They're not very big, but they're powerful. They'll go right uphill. Yeah, they go up them canyons. They got maybe five or six, six-sudden drivers on each side. And they got a rod running through. And this engine runs this rod, and the rod's got a worm gear to them. Yeah. And there's worm gear on every wheel, every wheel on the track that they drive with. I see. Something like a mallee. You know, old malleys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I never...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They're the same thing. Oh, yeah, the old malleys. They used to use them to go to the Rocky Mountains. Hmm. That's what they... Oh, they was using them old malleys back in 1936. I know I was coming down through Wyoming. Coming down off the... What they call the path going across there. Oh, what did they call that path going across the rock? It goes through the army and up through the... You can go over the rock. You'd not know you're going up here. Not going over a mountain. You ever been through that Lincoln Highway?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, you go up to Market, Utah, and you go up to Evanston, Washington. You go up to Wyoming. And then all the way through Wyoming, you're going over the Rocky Mountains. You can see for miles either way that you can't see a mountain. But you get up to the army, well, you're something like 7,000 some feet high. I know I... I always had that shampoo question, but I got into Rollins, Wyoming, and stopped and get gas. So I told that guy, I said, my gosh, they told me I left Utah this morning. I drive over the Rockies. I said, where the hell are they? I've been driving all day and I haven't seen a mountain yet. Man, he says, you're right on top of it. Great Divide, they call it. Great Divide. Going across the Rockies. Yeah, I drove all day and I haven't seen a mountain yet. He said, man, you're right on top of them. And it's something like 7,000 feet up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, here you've got no trouble. You know, you see it... At least it's either a hill or a mountain, but you see it. Oh, it's there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, you can see mountains way off in distance. Up there, you know, you could be going along there. There's only about three or four towns through Wyoming. You have Everson and I think the next one is Green River. And I forget the next one. It comes to Rollins, then Laramie. Over a hundred miles between towns. She was right off in Denslet. We went through in the wintertime and I went through there. And you could see a train way off, you know, over across the prairie there in the little... The Union Pacific runs through there. But you'd be going on the highway and way down... The real train looked like a little toy train. You could see so far and you could see that train just as plain but as little. It's just high altitude, I guess. There it's there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people from around here travel much? When they were in the early days?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, there's some of them, but there's a lot of them. Ninety percent of them are traveling just by the Spokane and back. Yeah, there's a few people that get out and travel around. I've done little travel. I've been in California, back in Kansas, Oklahoma, Colorado. I've been all through there in Montana. A lot of people of course travel a lot more than that. A lot of my guys haven't been any further than folk animal way from the country either. About like when I was a kid, it was kind of a backward family named Knox. Knox was up here. Two boys, one of them was Herb and the other was Fred. They was grown men but you'd go up there in an ordinary time, I don't know, I don't know if they ever had any shoes or not, but they'd always run around there barefooted, you know. And so one day they was downtown and they all dressed up and had shoes on and everything. My guys don't know where they're going all dressed up like that. My guys were going to Spokane to see them high buildings. I don't think they had those buildings in Spokane over six stories high. They're going to see them tall buildings up there. They're going to see the world. Yeah, they've been in Spokane to see the world.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to that family? Did they stick around?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, no, they're coming around here now. No. No, I don't know. The only one I know of now is, I guess he's still living, I don't know, but he was living in Lewiston. That's what I heard of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they just poor?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, everybody was poor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were no poorer?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I guess anybody that had a thousand dollars in them, they was a millionaire. So I don't know how poor you'd have to get, you know, to be completely poor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But most kids wore shoes though.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, well, I guess they could, I don't know what the idea, but they were the healthiest kids around the country.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Boy, that's...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they was healthy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess everybody, everybody was poor.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah, there was some guys maybe had a little bit more than somebody else did, but then, oh, they always had their big shots around the country. Some guy got a couple dollars more than the other guy, the other guy had to bow to him. They run the country around here. Yeah. So, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me, tell me what you're telling me about Laird again, because that reminds me of Laird. What? When you ran into Laird.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, Laird? Yeah. Oh, I don't know him for instance. Yeah. Oh, he had a chauffeur. He had his private chauffeur. Fucked around the neighborhood, not Floyd, but Albert Utes. And he had his uniform on, you know, and he was a regular chauffeur. All he had thought of his job was to keep his car clean and gassed up and just regular chauffeur John. When the boss wanted to go to Bovill, well, he'd drive him. He had a big Hudson Super Six. And, uh, it was a big car. And so, uh, that guy said he'd come up the road and that chauffeur would hold the road, you know. The road was just too late, you know, and you'd find a place to turn out, you know. You couldn't just go by with another sixty mile an hour like you do now. You had to look for a place to turn out and let the car go by you. Oh, my God, he'd just hold the road and put everybody out in the ditch or anything else where you had to get to keep them getting run over. Well, I was just a dumb kid. My dad had an old Billy car. It was new. It was an old make Billy. You ever heard of them? Billy B-E-L-A-E, Billy. And, uh, so, uh, I mean, there used to be a blue hall right there. You'd hit the highway. You'd push the rail track and I think they got an ice plant there now where they freeze meat. And then you'd hit the road and go right down through town. Well, the road was wide. It was wide as the street was. There was plenty of room. But there was actually just one road right down through town. Well, I turned that car over to the old Billy and I was way down there in town. I'd see the car just throwing dust out to beat the devil. I knew that old husband. I made my mind I was going to give him the whole road. I gave him half of it. So I put the two front wheels in one track and he was coming out the middle. And I just drove that old Billy wide open and right down there, I met him right down there. You know where the post office is I mentioned? It used to be a slough right there. It's across the post office. Between that and the railroad track, it used to be a slough. It was full of water all summer long. I used to skate on it when I was a kid, wintertime. And anyway, I held my road on him and he got right up there. I don't know, but all the months he seemed to be going out to give. Playin' chicken I guess you'd call it nowadays. And he'd give and he wound up over in that slough. He didn't upset or nothin'. He just plowed out there in that slough and then stuck out there and I kept going. I didn't even know how that happened until after I came back somebody told me, oh, well, I wound up out in that slough. Well, they had to get a team of horses. I got to pull him out of it. And he canned his chauffeur right there. He never helped out. So that he drove all the time and drove himself. Yeah, I sure broke that chauffeur from suckin' eggs. Like it got itself killed too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He ever knew that you did it?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, I don't know why. If he did, I never found out. I never sure as hell told him. Because I worked out the mill. He was super down at the mill. I never told him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it's funny. It's funny that the guy would do that. Just because he's got the money.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, he was a big shot. He'd walk down the streets of Potlatch and he wouldn't even see ya. He wouldn't even look at the speaker or nothin'. Everybody's supposed to. Why, it's been a crime to say all over the land, I guess. It's been an insult. But he mellowed out though before he died. He got to show you what it was quite like. And they used to have dancers down in the old gymnasium down there. Oh, the doctors and dentists and all the bosses around the mill. Their wives, they'd all dress up and they'd have what they called balls, you know. And they had a veranda up above where the common people, they'd go up there and you could go down on the floor and watch them dance. But you wouldn't dare go down and get on the floor with them. Hell, you wasn't allowed down there. You was just common people. Anyway, they kept that up till. I was down there. I was up on the dam veranda with a bunch of them up there. Somebody got a hold of them stink bombs and they'd throw them down on the floor. Well, you know what a stink bomb is. Well, anyway, broke up the dance. So anyway, I guess the high-mucklemen kind of got the message and they never had one of them balls after that. That was the end of that. That's where they stopped that ball business. Did they try to find out who did that? Oh, I guess, oh, there. If you could find out who done it, you just kept him. Nobody knew nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Nobody knew.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, it's like one time here, oh, about 20 years ago. You know where Hampton is up there? Well, the railroad track comes up and they had a little, oh, damn little culvert for the water to go through, you know, up above on the other side. They had the water and everything coming from Goldil down. Had to go under the railroad track. Well, they just had a little culvert there that wouldn't carry the water. So every high water we'd have with that whole flat there between Princeton and Hampton was just a lake. And, by gosh, you know, every time, so they tried to get that to build a bigger culvert down there. And they wondered that that water goes through. Well, they argued it wasn't a culvert's fault. It was a river covered up and pushed the water back. Well, it sounded a bit logical, but, so one day, anyway, a bunch of guys, I don't know who it was, they got down there one day and they put a bunch of dynamite in that culvert and they just blowed that damn thing all to heck. And they notified the company because they always run the logger up there early the next morning. So they called in down at the railroad yard down there and told them the culvert was out up there. So they brought the engine up the next morning and a couple of guys walked ahead of the engine until they come up before daylight, you know. They had their flight flight and they was walking up there and they finally come to it. And, oh, boy, the old, what the heck was the guy, head of the railroad business down there, I can't think of his name. But he, he's an up person, inquiring around who does, you know, nobody, everybody, well, at first everybody said, I did it and everybody said that. I did it well, in fact, you know what. The whole town did it, I did it. Well, I don't know who did do it. I never heard. I never did hear who did it. I think I know who does, but the funny part of it was Floyd Layton ended up on that hill there, that big square house up there. It was on his place where the culvert was. And he was aggravated because he had a place where his cows run down there, you know, and it was all flooded all the time in the high water. So anyway, he was awful nervous because it looked like if any guy would do it, it'd be him. But I don't think he had anything to do with it. He was pretty nervous about it. And anyway, by the time they was up there, they built a culvert down that carried the water. We hadn't had any high water over the road since. So it was the culvert that did it. Well, they got one down there now that carried the Mississippi River through there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess they didn't want any more trouble.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, well, so anyway, all the way around there, it's like we were out here at different times. Yeah, all Potlatch, you know, they just like the United States was a rural, you know. They could do anything they pleased. It's like old Nixon. Nobody could do anything about it because they had all the big lawyers, you know. You couldn't sue them. They could have done damage while you couldn't sue them because they had their own lawyers and everything. They pushed their weight around pretty strong. Like the United States had been doing the rest of the world. Any country that doesn't do what the United States says, they send the Marines down there and chew the hell out of them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: We kind of come to the end of the road on that, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I hope so.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, we ain't. I guess we don't dare to. It's like that Panama business they had down here a while back, you know. It's been ten years, twelve years ago, they sent a bunch of Marines down there and kicked the hell out of them Panamanians, you know, teach them a lesson. They didn't do it this time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, maybe we're starting to learn.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Maybe we're going to grow up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, we're growing.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, maybe we're... Oh, I don't know, Nixon going to China here five or six, seven years ago at the most. Why, the communists were a dirty communist. Everything you could hear was a dirty communist, you know. Communists were the cause of all the troubles in the world. Well, I told my wife one of these days it was going to turn out this way. We used to live up here on the river above Princeton up there, the place there. Used to be an old wagon road running through the brush going up to the other place. Kind of a shortcut through there. We had a little dog named Watch. I call him, we named him Watch. He's about two-thirds grown pup yet. We let dogs run alongside the wagon and a coyote come down over the hill. And of course the dog looked up and seen that coyote. So, oh boy, take out the coyote and the coyote broke and ran up the hill. Dog rung up their way. We were riding along the wagon and I watched it. So the pup run up the way and come back. Pretty quick the coyote come a little bit closer to the wagon. Pup took after him again. The dog went further. So then the dog come back to the wagon. Coyote was back there. Oh, twice before I made this television from the wagon, you know. And the dog took after him and the coyote broke out of there and ran like a level. And at that time the coyote dog changed his foot up the hill. But coyotes circle around and come back between the dog and the wagon. So, here's the funniest thing about that. You can see it talking about the expression on his leg. The thing with an animal, they got much expression, but they have. And that pup looked up and he saw that coyote between him and the wagon. So he didn't know what to do. Pretty quick he got down his belly and he started to crawl into the coyote to make up. He's going to be, ha ha ha, going to make up for that coyote. That old coyote, you know, you can see how foxed that dog was. He had his tongue out and just laughing. So anyway, we had to stop the wagon and put a stop to that circus. That's what little Nixon done. By gosh, he had to go to China and the old thing, if you can't whip them, join them. So he went to Russia. And the communists, you don't hear much about it anymore, do you?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: In fact, Mary Nixon says they're wonderful people. They're not dirty communists no more. They're wonderful people. So, that's the way that dog was. And they got you where you can't go no more, but they're ready to make up. Ha ha ha ha.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think that's, I really agree with your description. I think that's the way it is.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: By gosh, United States is going to push their weight around. One of these days they're going to have to do just like that dog. They'll be boxed in until that's what we're going to do. And so it happened. Yeah, it happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you think Potlatch is going to get that way too? Have they already?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they're on the end of the rope. They're going to put a little cedar mill in, but that's just a matter of time. There won't be no mill down here. Yeah, it's all washed up. Unless they get some kind of different industry, I don't know what it'd be. The lumber on the line, I don't know, is pretty well all logged off around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that's the real reason why Potlatch is closing down? Because all the lumber's really gone? Well... You think it's just that they want to make, they're not making enough money?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They, this mill was losing money. It wasn't making money, it was making out of other mills. And it was kind of a, oh, it was like a man got several farmers around, he got one by a guy that they produced and he'll peddle it. Same thing, they wanted to peddle this mill and kind of get out of it. It wasn't making money like some of the rest of them, so they wanted to phase it out. Yeah, I think that was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But how many people depend on that for your living?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: About 250, I think, men working down the mill until they, well, now and until June the first tenants shut down, you know. And they're going to remodel the mill over down there. I don't exactly know what they're going to do, make dimensions that way, I understand 2x4, 2x6's. They're going to put in a bunch of resaws. So anyway, they're laying off 140 men, the way I understand it. Of course, a lot of them have already got different jobs. I know my son, he was working down there and he was one of them that was going to be laid off. He went over to Garfield and that love outfit over there, he's working over there now. Their machine shop outfit. Oh, I don't know. Things change. Oh no, we're getting short of timbers. That's no joke. No, they're bound to. Why, they hauled this mill, they hauled, I don't know, I've met potluck trucks way up there. They're slightly clunky, way back there. You ever been back in that country? High country they call it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I've been up the St. Joe and up near the North Fork of the Clearwater Sound.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, you've never been to Clarkia?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I've been there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Back in behind there, oh, they got, oh, I don't know, Glover Creek and Diane's through there and they go clear back to the bowl cabins. That's the old big country back there. Well, that's pretty well logged off. Last time I was up there, they was, and that's not far from the Montana line when you get back in there. They just been pretty well logged in the Montana line, and played down here at this mill in order to get logs. So you can see when they get that far back to the logs, it's getting pretty scarce. Especially when I was a kid, you could start right out here, and, but they had a civilization. You'd get big pine trees, big trees, and everything all the way clear of the bowl. You'd just go through the forest all the way up through there. You wouldn't believe it. But, just what they call forest roads, you know. Big timber all the way along the road, all the way clear of the bowl. And then on up the up river, same way. Now there's no timber up through there. You can go up through there now and you can't see a tree by a guy's ticket. A mule couldn't pull out. It's just logged off. So there's no question about it. There's shorter logs. And it's a question the government stopped it. They could clear-tailed it down and give these mills all the logs they wanted all the time. But, anyways, a lot of guys are mad because they thought, well, these buck logs are running for ten years yet. Well, suppose, log it all up and then what have you got? It's only a matter of time. We didn't have to shut down anyway. And they run, saw the last tree down like they did the buffalo. You're out of buckles and you're out of logs. That's all you're doing. So I think the government's wise in doing what they did, and clear-tailed it back. Yeah. Well, we've got enough timber to keep this country from going, but I don't know whether they can just keep us out of boatload and boatload of lumber to Japan and all over the world like we've been doing all these times. We'd been like China, utilizing our raw materials just for our own use, but we'd have had everything we needed for thousands of years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that they would have been better off if they'd have regulated the timber cutting a long time ago? Why? Would it have saved the timber?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Why, you go over in Germany and they got the black forest over there. They got a lot of timber left by guys that we have. It's much different when you start it there. They cut a tree down, they had to plant two. And it's been going on for a thousand years that way, but they still got their forest. They didn't go and just flatter it like we did and just mutilate the forest and leave it in the little. No, the Germans would come over here and see the way we logged this land off and the shape we left it in and scream the high-hand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did people think about it, though, back in the early days when...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: They thought they'd have timber forever. Why, there was so much timber here, there wasn't enough saws to saw it all down in a thousand years. Yeah, they just thought there was no end to timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think that way yourself?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, I think so. Guys would go out by guys that saw good trees down. They didn't have, there wasn't no mills around they could haul logs to. They'd just get them pushed up in the pile and set fire to them in order to get a few acres to farm. Destroy the timber just to get a few acres to farm. Yeah, just timber didn't mean nothing. Lots of timber.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Boy, a lot of the land was cleared around here by the guys that we just hauled off in piles and burnt just to get us off the land so they could farm it.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I don't know. Gold's coming back in now and getting to be quite an item again. There's a lot of gold in that hill over there, what they call a gold hill. There's a lot of gold there. It's like somebody was buying it. It's bound to be. Bound to be a lot of gold. Them creeks. In fact, you can find colors in all them creeks. One of them lived up there on Meadow Creek. Well, the Pools River too. Used to run a bunch of chickens there, you know, barnyard chickens. And then once in a while she'd kill a chicken and get a couple of buckets of gold out of there. No food. You go out there, they don't know where the gold come from. And the mother loaded someplace up there, someplace, but they never found it. All you can do is just plastomize, you know, you can go up the creek and get a few nuggets out of there, you know. And same old chicken, they'd go through those cracks around them rocks and they'd find a bright piece of gold they used. Nobody ever got rich on it. Not yet. They'd get a big outfit in there so they could just tunnel right through that mountain, you know. They'd find gold. Oh, I think what happened, you see there's two hills. They got the East Gold Hill and the West Gold Hill, I think they called it. There's a saddle in between them. I think it was one big mountain at one time and she blowed up. And I think that's the reason them gold was all scattered all over the creek up there. Scattered all over it. I think it just blowed out. Otherwise, how'd that gold get slid down in them meadows down there above Harvard there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was right in the meadows.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. It was down next to the Blue River, down next to the meadows. There's a lot of gold right there in the Blue River. But anyway, that gold scattered. There's a lot of gold there someplace nobody's ever found. There's a big guy that dug holes in the mountain up there and mined. But both of it, they'd get a hole back in there and get a few little nuggets someplace and blow around and sell stock in that mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They sell stock around here or Spokane?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. There's a guy up here by the name of Pinnell. Oh, you go, you see the gold well. Oh, yeah. He got above Harvard. And I think the name of that creek you go up. But you go up there, you go up on the side of the Gold Hill. He had a mine up there. And he sold a lot of stock in that mine. And a lot of guys put a lot of money in that gall damned mine. But I don't, nobody'd want anything. They all lost their money. Old Pinnell was the only one that made any money out of it. He sold stock, took the money, got the heck out of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He never worked it after he sold the stock?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, not much. I don't think he done much work. He had to try to work it digging a hole back in. And he went selling stock in it. But he had gold to show you. But I kind of found out that gold didn't come out of Gold Hill. He had some gold bag houses from Nevada and Oregon. He'd show you gold bag houses. All that guy stuck it on. I didn't. He tried to get me, but he didn't make it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much would he get from a person? How much would he?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, depending on how much stock he bought.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much they wanted, huh?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, he'd say all stock he wanted. But I don't know what it was a share now. Something like five dollars a share or something. He'd buy so many shares. Oh, the old guy, he was throwing this place right here by this house above me here. Watson, old George Watson. By the guy, he just about broke him up in business. He was just so sure he was going to get rich in that, you know. He just about broke himself up in business by his talking. Gold's getting down. They're going to start mining gold now. And I hope they get it around so we can even buy some gold. You ever have your hand on a twenty dollar gold piece?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Never did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've had a little, couple little pieces of gold from the creeks in my hands, but nothing but...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, it used to be. I was down in Oregon. Oh, I was back in about twenty-four, I guess it was. Heck, you can take your paycheck in and catch it every weekend. And by the guys, you'd probably get maybe two twenty dollar gold pieces. Ten dollar gold pieces about the size of a dime. Yeah, you'd have twenty dollar gold pieces in my pocket. Dime. Ten dollars, five dollars. Five dollar gold pieces. They're just like any other money. They just slip through your fingers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'll bet if people could have gotten a hold of that guy, they would have given him a pretty hard time.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, they couldn't really prove nothing. No, he had a mind and it was all the good of it. Couldn't prove nothing against him. And what the hell he been doing? He didn't really have anything he could sue him for. I don't think he was going to come back. It's like all of my adventures, like old Barnum Bailey says, there's a sucker at every dang gun on the other team, you see. I forget how he called it. What did old Barnum say to him, what was his saying?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't remember, but I know just what you mean. Oh, I see.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: There was a sucker born every minute.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: That's it.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: There was a sucker born every minute.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Now you get stuck on it. I got stuck on it a hundred dollars worth. Had a mic in mine up here by Avon. Oh, it was a real mic. It had a hole dug in there and you could get big chunks of mic that thick, you know. And, well, it looked like a sure fire thing. So I stuck a hundred dollars in the mat. A lot of guys did. I don't know anyone. But the darn outfit, they sold stock here and I just kind of pulled it up. That was the end of it. They threatened to sue, but he never did anything about it. I don't know. Another company took it over and went over it and processed a bunch of mic out of it. And mic I guess didn't have no price on anymore. They come out and manufactured stuff. But all they used it for was like insulators. Mic is a good insulator in electrical appliances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they replaced that?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I think they got replacement for it now. Some kind of damn stuff they built. Anyway, mic I just didn't have no value so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this guy a local that had the mine?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, I don't know where he comes from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the mine up near Micah Mountain?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You know where Deary, Deary is? That mountain they call Fudd Hill is right on the back of that. You kind of go in north of Deary and you go in the back part of the mine. That's where the Micah Mine was. Fudd Hill. Yeah, that's where it was. Oh, it looked good. It was alright. I don't know. Anybody that hasn't got stuck back on mining stock again, been very much an old diver. Everybody got stuck on some time. I know my dad got old stock. Mining stock is worthless. I don't know whether it was around here yet or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most of the folks though, around here they didn't. When you were homesteading you didn't mine too.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, no. No, there wasn't too much mining went on. My dad first started, that's the first thing he did when he came out. He was mining up on Gold Hill. Oh yeah? Yeah, he, oh, he's like raised in Kansas. A couple of his friends come out here and, oh boy, they prospected to go on Gold Hill and they found a little gold. They wrote back and my dad and their brother, those three brothers, two of them come out and one of them came back so they're going to get rich out of their gold. So the very guy said, my dad and the other brother come back out here. And they had quite a system. You got to pay top dollar to get anything done for your neighbors nowadays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder why it changed.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh boy, I guess it changed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean I wonder why.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, greed I guess. Just like the whole United States changed, just greed. Everybody after that and a dollar or something, I don't know what it is, jealousy. Seems to be the thing, but I guess you can outsmart somebody else now and beat them out of something, that's a big deal. Used to call it crooks to do that, now it's just a big deal to just get somebody out of something for nothing.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well there's a couple of years before they got out there to just pop that. You ever been to Elk River? Way back when I was a young brother, they built a mill up there, saw mill, had three band saws. And then they rebuilt that mill and electrified the whole thing. Well, timber all around, well you know the timber they're hauling out there in that country down here now. But they had that mill all electrified, the most modern mill in the northwest. And everybody was up there, they went and built homes. You know, they thought they had to live there forever, all that timber around that one little mill. And bad guys, I don't think had run three years, all the once they shut it down, and tore it down and moved it out. Left guys to take up there with nice homes. I guess they hauled some of them home out to Moscow, sold them for a little to nothing. Some of them moved out to Moscow and they moved around the country. But that's just what pilots can do, you can't trust them as far as your old bull did it take.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they do that?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know, it just didn't make sense. Timber there, they tore the mill out, and had a river built right in there. Well, Milwaukee River run right into Elk River. It just don't make sense what they do, but that's what they done. Everybody thought they were sitting for life there, you know, there could be plenty of work there to rest their lives. They went and built some nice homes and fixed things up. All of a sudden they got an order to shut that mill down. And not only that, but they tore it down and moved it out. Then went to Holland and walked straight up in there and flew it down to Lewiston and down here. Don't make sense. And that's what they done. Yeah, they used to have those old dirt roads. And they didn't have no grades, you know, they didn't grade the daggone roads. They'd go down a hill, up a hill, down a hill, up a hill, all the way up to Boer and down to Elk River, same way. I had some friends, kids that I used to chase around and moved up there. They said, oh man, got a job upstairs. I had a bicycle. So I made my mind one Sunday morning, I was going to Elk River to see them kids. It was about 60 miles, I guess, the way the old road used to run. I think, guys, I started out that Sunday morning and it was just about time it was getting dark. That night I pulled into Elk River with a little bicycle. But that was the first time I ever had the bubble. I could have had money with me, I could have stopped in Boer to get something to eat. But from Elk River, Boer to Elk River, it was just a jungle all the way through there. There was no civilization or whatever, about 25 miles I guess through there. It was just a jungle. I didn't want to get caught in that jungle in the dark, so I didn't take time in Boer to get anything to eat. I got up there and there was mines up on the hill, I'll not leave a mountain there. I don't know what they were mining for, but they had a mine up there. They were letting their damn dredges all go into the creek, you know, and that water just runs yellow. It was hot in the middle of the day I was going through there. Gosh, I got thirsty, thirsty, thirsty. So, by gosh, I didn't want to drink that damn water, you know. But finally I went up along the creek there and I got pretty quick to the bridge when I crouched it and it started up that mountain. That was the end of the line, I guess I had to get a drink. So I went down and I took a pretty good swig of that old yellow water. I went back on the back and wheeled my bike across the bridge right on the other side of the sign that says fresh water. Nice spring right there. Oh boy. Well, I had to take a good drink of that to wash that little mud out of me. I went and fired me in that room down there by a guy. I just went out across the room and just turned there and there was a sign that said fresh water, spring water. I said I'd take a swig of that damn old rusty water. And I wheeled up the mountain, I had to walk up and push the bicycle over it and we got what they called a lever mountain. I got pretty up close to it and started running across the huckleberries. I was getting pretty daggone like, I think Lisa left home. So anyway I stopped along the side of the road there and picked a bunch of huckleberries. You know you can eat huckleberries and get hungrier all the time. Anyway I filled a pretty good huckleberry and I went up the hill pretty close to the top and I came across two or three cars parked there. People's milk were out there picking huckleberries. I got this one guy, he was just packing up his cart to get ready to go home. He said if you had anything left to eat, I'd buy it. He wasn't bummed, I'd buy it. He wanted to know where it was from, I told him. He said, from there today? I said yeah. He said you need something to eat. So he got his glove box out and gave me a couple of sandwiches. Boy, I just do this all live. Well after I got to the top of the hill, it was practically all downhill in Delgivre. I just sailed in Delgivre and nothing like that. That was the first time I ever had the bum. Boy, I didn't figure out bumming out of the place, but he didn't take no money. Kind of amused the darn kids right about to take a life out of them one day. Anyway I rode her up there, hung up for about a week out and then I rode her back home again. Nowadays, I got a kid that can't even ride a bicycle, he's a townie mack, we got to have motorcycles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do that week you spent there?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, just played around with the neighbor kids. Just farts around. Yeah, I think that was the weakest of three or four days, I don't know how long it was. I stayed a few nights in there, I know that. That was right after World War I. I know that one of the guys that was coming back from the war in Germany made a French girl. And they was living there in town. I didn't know the friends up there, they'd tell me about that French woman. Well, I didn't have no idea what the hell a French woman looked like, so I just had to see her. So they had the old building with woodsheds, you know. We got down, we sneaked around there and laid down to watch until finally she come out. Hence she looked like any other woman. I don't know what I was looking for. She looked like any other woman. Because she was a French woman, I don't know what I was looking for, but she had to be something different. And I was pretty disappointed and she looked like any other woman. Oh, I don't know. Funny how small the world is though. I remember one time I was up at Yellowstone Park. And we was watching one of them bubbling springs there and they had a cavalry raiding around there. And I was leading against raids and the old fellas in Staten Island. I bet there was 50,000 people in that park that day. All scattered all over. Big crowd everywhere you go, you know. I was standing there and finally I got to talk to that guy. He wasn't aware I was from, I told him, Prince is down here. Oh, we are. He said, you want to know if I knew Pete Packy, John and Hart, much to them. I said, yeah. I said, they're down here, yeah. And come to find out he worked for the company. His job was building these bunk houses. Logging out, bitch, no longer building my own railroad cars. That was his job, worked for a Potlatch and built bunk houses. And another time I was going back to Kansas. Little town there, just a little burg at that time. I guess it's a pretty nice place now. Oh, uh, Fort Collins, Utah, Colorado. Just a little one service station town. I was real dying there. A woman come out and pumped some gas for me. She had a Idaho license. So, oh, she said you're from Idaho. I said, yeah. She said, I have a girlfriend that lives in Idaho. She married a guy by the name of Frank Off. Oh, nobody knew him. I said, yeah. He said, crow flies about a mile and a half, just right over the hill here where they lived. Just because of Midler, they could live in Boise, you know. I was from Idaho, so I just had to know if it happened to a friend. It happened to me. I knew him. Small world.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, when I get back there and people think of Idaho, I don't know what they thought of it, but you should know everybody lived in Idaho, if you lived in Idaho. It isn't real Idaho. Idaho is about 350 miles long. From the Canadian border to Utah, I guess it is about 350 miles in. So does it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do at Potlatch? What job did you...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I worked in the sawmill. I started out picking engines and things like that. And I worked up, got on the carriages and I set ratchets and finally wound up sawing, running the head saw. You know what a sawyer is in the sawmill? You've been in them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've been in them, but only just to look. I never worked in one.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, you know the guy that runs the carriage back up and down the line and saws the boards off? Well, that's the sawyer. He loads the logs and turns the logs and calls us. Got a setter, but now they got him out of the bank. Now they haven't got no carriage riders on them now down here. But at that time he had, my first time, he had what I call a dogger and a seller. Two men on a carriage. And the sawyer runs the carriage up and down, turns the logs and nigger. You know what a nigger is? No. Well, it's what they call a coon. It's a rod with teeth on it. Got a couple of teeth like this hanging out on it. About three, three, four teeth down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And it sets down, sets off two cylinders, team cylinders. And it's sitting down there and you run your log back over like this. Well, then you want to turn that log, you just pull it and run the nigger. The nigger comes up and turns that log over and goes back down the hole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That's what they call a nigger. Of course, they got different kinds. They got simons and turgers there. They turned the log with a hill nigger like they had down here. It just turns the log that way all the time. Turns it over. And the simons and turgers, the nigger's got a hook on it. You throw that hook over on top of the log and just log back. And then they got the loaders. All that does is pull the log back and the loaders come up and shove the log back up against the carriage. That's what they call a simons and turgers. They use that down on the coast on them big loggers. They got that big log there, boy. You wouldn't believe it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've seen them. I've seen them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You've seen them big logs?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've seen what they left of them, what they still got standing. They're big.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, I was a carriage man's seller. I didn't know what. East is east and west is west. But I went to Portland. Of course, I went and applied for a job with Sutton Ranchers. Well, they didn't have a...wanted to know my dog, but they had three men on that carriage. And they had a big log carriage on them. As long as the house is wide, I guess. And they had blocks on the carriage about that high. About as high as it was down here. But then they saw a little log down here. Well, the third dog, that track rung, they opened the doors up in the end of the mill and the track ran out over the Willamette River. And I didn't know it. I got on there and, of course, the dog was all kind of relics, you know. You had to pull the pin and set the dog up, and then put the pin in and pull down a lever to dog the log. Well, down here they had a...what they call a bark dog. They had a bunch of teeth in it that looked like this all down the block. A whole bunch of them. You just pull down a lever and them teeth would drive the log, you know, and hold it. Anyway, that man. And I used these rigs down here fast. And you had to hang on to something all the time. And them rigs are big logs that go slow, you know, and you can cut loose and walk. But in the carriage, like one of your blocks is here, you had the dog down there, and as far as that wall was, you'd walk over and the dog hit. But bad guys, I'd been hanging on this carriage longer. I just couldn't get loose. Just made you hang on. Anyway, I had a hell of a time. But the first log went out. The carriage was sitting there in there. And so they run the rig back way out over the...over them logs that's 40 feet long, I guess. Maybe longer than that. I don't know how long they were. They run straight out over the end of the mill. And a track was built out in the open air out there. Well, I didn't know that was going to happen. I was on the tail end. It was what they call the second dogger. And so I got out of the alley and just about tied the whistle loose and got to come down and open the door up in the end of the mill. Way we went out, straight out over the river, I looked down at the river right down below me. And of course, they had the log back out there where the log come rolling down. It's even kind of like I say, the blocks are only about that high. Nothing between me and the damn river. If I just knew them damn blocks would have stopped that log, you know, I'd just come rolling out every time. It was a bit big down. I bet the damn thing was eight foot thick. So I tell you, my air was down. That was them blocks stopping all right. Oh boy, I was ready to head for Idaho. I had no food. You just imagined back. I wasn't expecting that, you know. My guy, they opened that window up and the way we went out, straight out over the river out there, nothing but a track, you know, just a cap one. But behind me, I guess, was that log that come over that box, right? I'd have to make it a high dive. So my guys put the block stopped at all right. I worked half a day. That was enough for me. A bunch of dudes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I went and got a job in construction. Yeah. I went and got a job on construction. I stayed on construction once in a while down there. Working on them tall buildings. Oh, there we go. No, you can't just down here, you know, hell, they just fly up and down the track. Heck, you know, you're on steam once you have a shotgun feed. Shotgun feeds a good big cylinder. Well, if the track was 40 feet long, that cylinder would be 40 feet long, you see. And the rod was passing on to the carriage and this piston was in the cylinder and that piston would travel up there 40 feet in that cylinder, you know. And you had steam and throttle on both ends. You give it the steam on this end and force the carriage back and reverse it and the steam was shooting on this end and the other would be exhaust. It would just go back and forth like a shotgun feed. You down here, you know bad guys, they turn the logs down to fly and load them on the fly. If you had to stop the rig and tear the logs, you had to goof up some way. Oh, no, you just go back there and tear them logs down to fly and the carriage never stopped. Down there, you know, them big logs, hell, they had to be feeding through the sauce load. And down there, down at Redwood, down there in Scotia, California, when it took me a while down there, I saw them. And, man, like guys, they bring logs in there as high as the ceiling, you know, thick. They looked like a block of wood. They was damn thick in the 60s, but logs, they were about as thick as they were long. You know, it looked like a damn block. That's Redwood. Well, I don't know, I see the train coming there to Eureka and they'd have just one log on a black car. Maybe the next log would have maybe two or three logs on it. But there's just a lot of cars on that train, just one big log on a black car. You've seen the Redwoods?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, they're beautiful.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I don't know how they are now, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's left of them?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: What's left of them? I find the one first time I've seen them, oh, they'd been cleaning land off. They had some machine out there, they'd pull them stuff out, they'd log it off there. But you could see the forest down ahead of you there. I guess that was reserved. They couldn't log them, they'd sort of fire down, and that was the end of it. But anyway, oh, the sun was shining bright, you know, and I was batting right along and the highway ran right in that forest. Boy, even the guys I had to find one of the breaks. The types that go in there, you couldn't see nothing, just almost dark in there, you know. Them trees are tall, the sunlight can get in there. Some couldn't get down there at all. Big ferns growing out there, taller than you was, you know, ferns all over. You'd walk up one of them trees, my guys, they made you feel like you was just falling out. We went in and seen two trees. One right beside the other one was General Sherman and the other one was General Gratter. They were the two trees and they had a fence built around them. You'd probably seen them too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I haven't seen them, but I heard of them.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And we went and looked at that. They had a sign up there to give them a scale, them logs, you know, the biggest ones. And we wrote it down. And so anyway, it was too big a scale, 300 and some thousand board feet in one tree. So I come back one night and I was down in the mill, eating dinner down in the pond and talking about them. I said, well, I've seen one tree down there by a guy's that's more lumber scale than this damn mill was put out in eight hours. Oh, I thought I was spreading it on. I said, damn, there's one tree down there that's got more lumber scale than this damn sawmill down here. It'll put out in eight hours. And I got the figures right when I said it was across from here, they were looking for something. So I wrote them down. And it's a fact, they had 300 and some thousand log scale. And this mill was doing damn good to put up 250,000, you know, good days run. Saw hundreds and hundreds of logs. That tree was 23 and a half feet, something like that, through it. That's six inches, eight to ten inches, something by the guy. It was 23 and a half feet straight through the log.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess, though, that our white pine here is a wood that can't be beat for being a good wood.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Good, yeah, white pine. Yeah, it's good wood. It's getting off the scarce, though. Yeah, pretty scarce, white pine is one. But anyway, now that's how big that tree was. Then I got a picture around here. I had an old Dodge, a four-dollar forest sedan. A tree down there you can drive your cars through, you know. Yeah, I got a picture of an old Dodge sent me in that tree. And that tree was hollow. Fire had burned it. And it was just a chimney right up through the middle. And yet, outside of it was green. Yeah, there was limbs on it. It was a green tree. But then it was just like you could get inside and look up and it was just like a stove thing. And you drive in there and you can open all four doors on the sedan. And there was a tree on both sides of the car. They had this whole chisel through but the road was through there. There was nothing left but a tree on both sides of that wall to hold that tree up. Yeah, just wouldn't believe it until you see it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Seems like most people don't hardly know about the kinds of forests that we used to have. They used to be around here. They never got the attention but they were beautiful. That's what I've seen in the pictures.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know. I guess they got that pretty well logged off. Old LBJ's wife, what did they call her?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was a ladybird.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, a ladybird. She went back there and put on a quiet drive to save the redwoods. And she went out there. They got a part down there now that they can't go and cut. The sawmills fought the heck out of it. Why? What are they going to do? Log the lap or not? Like old Ronald Reagan did it? You seen one? You seen them all? Yeah, it was a crazy dark forest. When I was down there you drove into that forest. And I don't know. Seems like I drove through there mile on mile on mile. And the road, it was big trees there. They wouldn't cut the tree out of the road. They'd drive back the road right around the tree. You'd just take it around and down through there. They wouldn't cut a tree down. Maybe a small one or something they had to. But the road just makes it around through there all the way down. Come to a tree, they wouldn't cut that tree down. They'd go around the tree. The old highway would have fallen down. They got probably a four lane highway down through there now. Straight in the street. Yeah, I had to make. Got a four lane highway down through there. Where it was down there, just two lane road. A cabin road. And by the time you got to the tree it went around it. Oh, I don't want to see it again. I'd rather remember it the way it was. I have no desire to go back and see what's left. But anyway, we went miles and miles and miles through that forest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how long, do you know how long it was that they had the virgin timber here? The virgin timber here, the first growth before they started having to start cutting the second growth?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, that was all first growth timber they took off around here. That's when I was a kid. That was all original old growth timber. And when they got that locked off, that was about it around here. Everything else was, around here was all second growth. I don't know of any place around here that got any old timber anymore. Any original. What they call the old growth timber. Big old pine tree right down below my barn, that's old growth. It's bovine, it's a typical bovine down there. That's a pretty good size tree you can add to it. It's a butt. Yeah, it's a big tree. Yeah, that's one of the oldest trees around this back in the woods. The tree right down there. It's an old red one. That willow out there is quite a treat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's a beauty.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Take a look at how big that thing is.

words: I can see it, it's beautiful.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Spread it out all over. I bet it covers a half acre. That was the window, you can see it all. Take a look back there.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You get out of it, boy, it's like under a canopy because it's all leaped out. It gets all leaped out. It's only about half leaped out now. It will be when it gets probably leaped out. You can go out there by that level, it will be hanging down. It's almost dark under that thing.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And that willow's about it. Oh. Probably 80 years old. I can remember it when it was just a small bush, you know, just a small, oh, when I was a kid. Old road used to go by here and I used to go buy horseback. People lived here when the sun had TB. And they'd keep them out in fresh air. They moved him and made a bed for him under that willow tree out there. But it was just a small tree then. And I used to stop there and BS with him when he was laying out there. He finally died with TB. But that's just when I was a kid, so it was a pretty good sized bush, so I was about 80 years old. I can make a rough guess. I know the older I am, I'm 70. So it's been there a long time.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I know that the male, you know, they used to have what they call yellow pine. And it's a species all by itself, really, but down in the male they call it ponderosa. And that includes bull bull pine. And then they had another pine about halfway between that and the other. It was straighter, slimmer. Didn't have heavy limbs on like that one. They used to call them bastard pine. I don't know why, but it's bastard pine. Then they had the yellow pine. And I know my brother-in-law, he was one of the big bosses down in the male for years. He's retired now, but he argues that bull pine, and he's using bastard pine, are all yellow trees, yellow pine, when they get grilled up. When they get full-grilled, but it'd be yellow pine. Tell me, that old tree down there, there would be a yellow pine? You can tell them yellow pine, you see them in the woods, the bark's yellow. And they had all the difference in the world. So he finally asked me, he said, well, did you ever see a young yellow pine? I said, you're damn right, I got some on my place. The tree's about so big around, you just look down through the woods, and they're just yellow, you know. So I told him, sometimes you get a chance, I said, I'll show you a young yellow pine. I said, that bull pine and bastard pine all around them. He said, that's a yellow pine. I said, you'll have to admit, boy, that they're a different species entirely. Well, you take a yellow pine, they used to have a pitch, gum, you know, and you take a old yellow pine, you find a good odd chunk of that pitch, and dry it up, and you can chew that. Boy, that was good chewing. I mean, that was gum, as long as kids used to have the gum in them. Get up and find a yellow pine tree, and it's like gum on it. You can just chew it, chew it, chew it, you know, never chew the flavor out of it. But you go down and get some pitch off one of these bull pines, and boy, it'll crack you in. Oh, boy. And then the pine cones are all a different. I don't know, you remember when you was around the woods, of course, you're not old enough to remember back how far, but pine squirrels, they'd go up in them big yellow pine trees, and they'd cut them pine cones off, and they'd fall down, and then they'd come down, and you'd see a whole bunch of that pine cones, and they'd just tour it all to pieces, get them seeds out. You'd see a pine cone there on the ground just all tore up. Well, they lent them seeds. I used to turn them up and eat them seeds too. They'd take a big old pine cone, a yellow pine, and they made them fly. They got a wing on them, as the seed has, and then there's seeds right down the butt of it here. But when the pine cone gets plumb right, they're going to break loose and go flying through the woods, you know, in the wind. Just blow them out, and old pine squirrels, they used to hoard them for winter.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they'd hoard them things. They'd forget half the place where they hoarded them. But anyway, I know the poor service wasn't that. I didn't think I was right. They'd set crews out in the woods fighting them squirrels in order to get seed to plant forest with. Yeah, they used to have people running around in the woods trying to find them squirrels, where they'd catch the seed and rob them. That's a dirty trick. They would work all summer. Of course, they probably had a hell of a lot more catches than they could eat, but they'd just work all summer long. They'd catch all them seeds.

Yellow pine, when the pine cones get right, they'd spread out. And you'd get down there, you know, and you'd dig them little... I don't know whether you'd call it part of a statement sticks out, but the seeds would be down inside of that. Just break them off and you'd get the seeds out. Just break them back and shake them and the seeds would fall out. Oh, I used to shell them out and get a handful and eat them. I can see why the squirrels like it. It's good.

The only place I know there's any yellow pines up on my place, up on the other place up there. It's just a few trees, and I know they're yellow pine. But I don't know why they didn't...they never received it and come back like the other pine did.

words: What's the difference in the appearance of the tree between a yellow and a bull if you're just looking at the tree straight?

words: Well, it thickens the bark, the color of the bark. And then, of course, if you've got a tree cut down, yellow pine will have a heart. It'll be about that much sap on a big yellow pine log, about that much sap right around the rest of its whole heart. It's just about so much sap. And then you think the bathroom pine, they're halfway between a bull and a yellow pine, but they'll have a heart about that big around and so much sap.
